reasoning:
The baseline trajectory for US bank reserves is downward due to the Fed's ongoing Quantitative Tightening (QT), which is currently running at around $20 billion per month. In addition, the Treasury is projected to rebuild the Treasury General Account (TGA) to ~$850 billion by the end of June 2025. Because the Overnight Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP) is largely depleted, these liquidity drains will directly impact and reduce commercial bank reserves. 

For the short-term horizon (May 18), week-to-week volatility is high, meaning a random one-week bump has roughly a near-coin-toss probability (46%). For the 1-month (June 10) and 3-month (Aug 9) horizons, the combined effect of the TGA rebuild and persistent QT applies strong downward pressure on reserves, driving the likelihood of an increase versus the May 11 anchor increasingly downwards. For the 6-month horizon (Nov 7), although structural draining continues, uncertainty inherently increases. At this range, there is a higher probability that the Fed could halt QT entirely to maintain the Lowest Comfortable Level of Reserves (LCLoR) or respond to macroeconomic shifts, injecting liquidity back. Thus, the 6-month probability is adjusted back closer to 0.5 compared to the August horizon, fulfilling the rule of increasing uncertainty with longer horizons.
